merged_findings: Destination Port distribution in the DDoS class is highly concentrated and stable, characterized by zero entropy and a dominant ratio of 1.0, indicating a narrow structural pattern with strong class-conditioned separation from the BENIGN class.
BENIGN class shows moderate entropy and dominant ratio, reflecting more variability in Destination Port usage.
High Jensen-Shannon divergence between DDoS and BENIGN classes confirms strong class separation in Destination Port distribution.
Destination Port exhibits stable, low-entropy dominant values with consistent class-conditioned separation across time segments.

Moderate shortcut-like predictive leverage of Destination Port is indicated by one-feature decision stump analysis, supporting the presence of representation-sensitive shortcut signals, though the full extent remains uncertain due to limited analysis complexity.
No local evidence of correlation or dependency between Destination Port and other features in the DDoS class was found within the bounded context of evidence e33.
open_gaps: Shortcut verification limited to one-feature decision stump; more complex feature interactions and broader dependencies remain unexamined.
Analysis limited to local evidence e33 and bounded actions; potential correlations outside this scope are unresolved.
Moderate signal strength leaves some uncertainty about the full extent of shortcut-like leverage.
update_focus: Destination Port distribution stability and shortcut-like predictive leverage within DDoS class under bounded local evidence
